title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Marine Megafauna andClimate Change Modeling Workshop


Save the Blue Five | Santa Cruz, Galápagos | June 8-12, 2026


Climate projections, biological data readiness, and first-generation species distribution models




Isaac Brito-Morales, PhD June 2026

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
section: Shared framing: how to read the models
text:
Shared framing: how to read the models


Shared framing


How to read the models


Before using maps or species distribution model outputs, we need a shared way to interpret them



Projections are “what-if” futures


Uncertainty comes from the full workflow


Species distribution models estimate habitat suitability, not certainty


Outputs are decision-support tools, not final answers

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Projection not prediction

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)

Projection ≠ prediction


Prediction
“This is what will happen”


Projection
“This is what could happen under these assumptions”


Conceptual schematic adapted from Schoeman et al. (2023), Trends in Ecology & Evolution doi:10.1016/j.tree.2023.04.005




For this workshop, climate projections help us explore plausible futures, not identify one exact future

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Where uncertainty enters

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)

Where uncertainty enters

Uncertainty comes from the full chain





Uncertainty is not failure; it tells us where to be cautious

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Climate-side uncertainty

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)

Climate-side uncertainty


Scenario: which future climate pathway?e.g., SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5


Climate model: which global model produced the projection?e.g., Earth System Models or Global Climate Models from modeling centers such as GFDL, IPSL, CESM, and others


Run or ensemble member: which realization of the model?e.g., r1i1p1f1, r2i1p1f1, or other ensemble members depending on the model archive


Downscaling approach: how do we translate coarse projections to the regional scale?e.g., delta method, statistical downscaling, and dynamical downscaling


Depth and variable layer: which part of the ocean and which variable?e.g., surface, mid-water, and seafloor layers for temperature, oxygen, salinity, currents, and related predictors

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
section: Biological and species distribution model uncertainty
text:
Biological and species distribution model uncertainty

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)

Biological and species distribution model uncertainty

Records: where do we have data, and where are gaps?
Sampling bias: absent, or just not sampled?
Predictors: which variables define the niche?
Algorithm: which statistical approach?
Suitability: suitable does not always mean present, abundant, or critical habitat

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
What species distribution models show

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)

Species distribution models estimate environmental suitability
They do not directly show:


confirmed presence
abundance
reproduction
population trends
management priority by themselves



Suitable ≠ occupied ≠ important habitat

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
How we use the outputs

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)

Use model outputs as decision-support
They can help identify:

possible climate exposure
future suitable areas
data gaps
areas needing monitoring
places where planning may need to be adaptive


Interpret outputs with expert knowledge, local context, and governance feasibility

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Workshop rule

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)

The goal is not to find “the perfect map”
The goal is to ask:

What patterns are consistent?
What looks uncertain?
What needs expert review?
What can improve in the next version?

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
section: Why use species distribution models for Blue 5?
text:
Why use species distribution models for Blue 5?

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Why use species distribution models for Blue 5?

Species distribution models are useful here because they integrate species information with physical and biogeochemical ocean conditions to estimate where suitable habitat occurs now, and how that suitability may change under climate change



Species records


+


Ocean conditions



→


Habitat suitability


→


Climate change impacts



Species distribution models link species occurrence records with the ocean conditions that shape habitat suitability
They allow us to translate physical climate change into potential biological responses
For Blue 5, this helps identify where suitable habitats may persist, decline, or shift in the future

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
section: Our baseline species distribution model framework
text:
Our baseline species distribution model framework

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Our baseline species distribution model framework

We use an ensemble species distribution modeling approach that combines multiple algorithms, from classical statistical models to machine-learning methods

Ensemble of 10 algorithms, including GLMs, GAMs, MaxEnt, Random Forest, and Boosted Regression Trees


\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{logit}(p_i) =
&\alpha +
\beta_T T_i +
\beta_S S_i +
\beta_O O_{2i} +
\beta_{Chl} Chl_i +
\beta_V V_i \\
&+
\beta_F FrontFreq_i +
\beta_U Upw_i +
\beta_E ENSO_t +
\varepsilon_i
\end{aligned}
\]



State variables

Temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, chlorophyll, and current velocity



Ocean variability predictors

Ocean front frequency, upwelling, and ENSO




Conceptual representation of the predictors used to estimate probability of occurrence or habitat suitability; this is not the exact fitted formula for every algorithm in the ensemble

Physical modeling and downscaling framework

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Physical modeling and downscaling framework

This section summarizes the ocean products and workflow logic used to connect present-day baselines and future climate conditions to species distribution model predictors

Contemporary ocean conditions and future climate projections
Horizontal and vertical products relevant to Blue 5 species
Delta/change-field downscaling to trusted baseline products
Curated layers designed for visualization and species distribution model extraction
Viewer: Ocean downscaling Shiny app

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
section: What is a physical climate model?
text:
What is a physical climate model?

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


What is a physical climate model?



Physical models represent the ocean, atmosphere, land, and ice on numerical grids; they simulate energy and mass exchange through time, producing variables that can be summarized for ecological modeling

horizontal grid
vertical layers
physical and biogeochemical processes
present-day and future time windows

text:
Future work for physical products

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Future work for physical products

The current viewer and pipeline provide a transparent foundation; the next step is to expand the ensemble and make uncertainty easier to interpret

More model families
More climate scenarios
More realizations / ensemble members
More depth-layer and pelagic summaries
Clearer uncertainty summaries for species distribution model interpretation

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
section: Status of biological data for species distribution models
text:
Status of biological data for species distribution models

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Status of biological data for species distribution models

The biological data workflow preserves, harmonizes, quality-controls, and exports telemetry and observation records before species distribution modeling

Raw data are kept auditable and unchanged
Telemetry and observations are standardized separately
Model-ready files preserve provenance and quality-control decisions
Final model inputs keep marine species distribution modeling-compatible coordinates
Viewer: Megafauna data viewer Shiny app


Local reference: shared/references/repos/pre-sdm-bio-data-prep-readme.md

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Data readiness is not uniform

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Data readiness is not uniform

The goal is to make data strengths and limitations visible before modeling decisions are made

Stronger data support more confident model interpretation
Sparse data require simpler or more cautious interpretation
Biased sampling can shape apparent habitat relationships
Expert feedback is needed to prioritize realistic improvements



42,475 filtered records in viewer


8 Blue 5 species in current app view


71 data sources


2 data types: telemetry and observations



isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

section: First generation of species distribution models
text:
First generation of species distribution models

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


First generation of species distribution models

These outputs are preliminary to intermediate products intended to guide the next phase of model improvement

Present initial model outputs
Review performance and ecological plausibility
Identify limitations and assumptions
Separate data gaps from model gaps
Prioritize realistic improvements over the next year


isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Interpreting first-generation outputs

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Interpreting first-generation outputs

When reviewing early species distribution model outputs, focus on whether patterns are ecologically plausible, data-supported, and useful for planning conversations

What patterns seem robust?
What patterns need caution?
Where does expert knowledge disagree with the model?
Which improvements would most strengthen final outputs?


isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Feedback should be specific

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Feedback should be specific

The most useful feedback identifies what can realistically improve the final outputs within the project timeline


Data feedback

missing sources
spatial or temporal gaps
questionable records
species-specific sampling issues



Model feedback

implausible spatial patterns
missing predictors
scale or depth concerns
uncertainty communication

text:
Future work

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Future work

The next phase should expand uncertainty representation and strengthen the connection between model outputs and conservation decisions

More climate models
More climate scenarios
More realizations and ensemble summaries
Better uncertainty propagation into species distribution models
Improved biological coverage and sampling-bias treatment
Clearer interpretation guidance for planning use

Discussion questions



Which data gaps matter most for the project timeline?
Which species or regions should be prioritized?
Which uncertainties must be communicated most clearly?
What outputs would be most useful for conservation planning?

title: SB5 Climate Species Distribution Model Presentations
text:
Take-home message

IKI - Save the Blue 5 (SB5)


Take-home message

The workshop is not only about showing model outputs; it is about making the workflow transparent enough that experts can improve the data, interpret the models responsibly, and identify the next most important steps


Shared framing


Physical products


Biological data readiness


First-generation species distribution models
